items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.q: What is the complexity of merge sort?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: O(n log n). “O(n)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which describes accessing an array element by index?
items.why: It's O(1). “O(log n)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 10 value. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
